does: This paper identifies and characterizes a critical failure mode where language and vision models suppress reporting of safety-critical information when task-conditioned, despite being capable of detecting that same information when unconstrained. The authors conduct systematic experiments across radiology text, driving scenarios, and chest X-ray vision tasks, demonstrating that conditioning on a narrow task causes models to omit hazard reports they would otherwise generate. They measure this "Inattentional Gap" across multiple model families and scales, finding the suppression persists regardless of model size, appears in reasoning models, and varies more by model family than by model parameters. The core contribution is empirically documenting this dissociation between performance on specified evaluation benchmarks and actual safety-relevant behavior in deployed settings.
connects: This work builds on research into model capability versus alignment misalignment and task conditioning effects in neural networks. It extends prior observations that models possess capabilities they don't reliably deploy, drawing connections to human inattentional blindness but mechanistically distinct. The paper responds to limitations in current AI safety evaluation practices that measure benchmark performance on explicitly specified hazards, building on growing awareness that such evaluations may not predict real-world safety. It relates to work on specification gaming, goal misgeneralization, and the challenges of comprehensive safety testing where unanticipated hazards dominate failure modes. The findings connect to broader research on how objective functions and task framing shape model behavior in ways that decouple from actual capability.
enables: This work opens avenues for developing evaluation methods that better capture real-world safety by testing models against a diverse, unanticipated hazard set rather than only specified ones. It motivates research into mitigation strategies for task-induced suppression, such as alternative prompting approaches, architectural modifications, or training procedures that preserve multimodal hazard detection. The paper suggests future work on understanding the mechanistic roots of why conditioning suppresses reporting, which could inform more robust safety alignment techniques. It points toward the need for safety frameworks that account for capability-deployment mismatches and specification gaming. The findings also motivate research into evaluating whether scaling or architectural choices can reduce the Inattentional Gap, and whether systems can be designed to maintain simultaneous awareness of multiple safety-critical signals even under task pressure.
